content.text: Every modern browser ships a Constraint Validation API: a set of HTML attributes, DOM methods, and CSS pseudo-classes that together enforce rules on form inputs without a line of JavaScript. Understanding this built-in system is valuable even in Angular apps, because Angular's own Validators mirror its semantics, and the novalidate attribute Angular adds to forms explicitly opts out of the browser's default UI so Angular can take full control.

attrs.alt: HTML Constraint Validation API — validation attributes, ValidityState flags, CSS pseudo-classes, and Angular interaction
attrs.title: HTML Constraint Validation Reference

content.text: Constraints are declared directly on the input element. The browser evaluates them whenever the value changes and when the form is submitted.
Value is not empty (not empty string, null, or undefined)
text, email, password, search, url, tel, textarea
Length of trimmed value is at least N characters
Length does not exceed N (browsers also block over-length typing)
number, range, date, time, datetime-local, month, week
Numeric/date value is not below min
Numeric/date value is not above max
Value is min + N*step for some integer N
text, email, password, search, url, tel
Full value matches the given regex
email, url, number each imply a format check

<!-- required: field must not be empty -->
<input type="text" name="username" required />

<!-- minlength + maxlength on password -->
<input type="password" name="pw" minlength="8" maxlength="64" required />

<!-- min + max + step on numeric -->
<input type="number" name="qty" min="1" max="100" step="1" required />

<!-- pattern: UK postcode -->
<input type="text" name="postcode"
       pattern="[A-Z]{1,2}\d[A-Z\d]? \d[A-Z]{2}"
       placeholder="SW1A 1AA" required />

<!-- type="email" enforces @ + domain -->
<input type="email" name="email" required />

<!-- type="url" enforces scheme (http:// etc.) -->
<input type="url" name="website" />
uses a JavaScript regex. The whole value must match — the browser wraps it in ^ and $ implicitly. Write without outer slashes.
silently prevents typing beyond the limit on text inputs. It does NOT stop paste of a longer string in some browsers — validate on submit too.
for date types accept ISO 8601 format strings: min="2025-01-01".
are excluded from constraint checking — validity is always valid for them.
Every form control has an input.validity property that returns a live ValidityState object. Each flag on it corresponds to a specific constraint failure. All flags are read-only booleans.
required is present and value is empty
type is email or url and value doesn't match the format
pattern is set and value doesn't match the regex
minlength is set and value is shorter (only when value is non-empty)
maxlength is exceeded (rare — browsers usually block over-limit entry)
min is set and numeric/date value is below it
max is set and numeric/date value is above it
step is set and value is not a valid step multiple
Browser cannot convert the input to a value (e.g. partial number entry)
setCustomValidity() was called with a non-empty string
True only when ALL other flags are false
event on the element when it fails. Listening to this event lets you intercept the browser's default bubble UI.
scrolls to and focuses the first invalid element in addition to showing the native tooltip.
and check every form control inside the form in order.
Use setCustomValidity() to flag a constraint not expressible with HTML attributes — for example a server-side uniqueness check or a cross-field rule.

Always call setCustomValidity('') (empty string) before re-running checks. If the flag is set to a non-empty string and you never clear it, the field remains permanently invalid even after the user fixes the value.
The browser keeps CSS pseudo-classes in sync with the ValidityState object. You can style valid and invalid inputs without any JavaScript.

:user-invalid was introduced in CSS Selectors 4 to solve the most common pain point with :invalid: it only matches after the user has interacted with the field (typed, blurred, or submitted the form). This prevents the jarring red-border-on-page-load that :invalid causes for required fields.
Please enter a valid email address.

The novalidate attribute on a form element disables all browser-native validation UI — constraint checking still runs, but error bubbles are suppressed and the form can be submitted programmatically even if invalid. This is how Angular takes over form validation.

<!-- No browser validation bubbles — Angular handles it -->
<form novalidate>
  <input type="email" name="email" required />
  <button type="submit">Submit</button>
</form>
only affects the UI — the validity flags on inputs are still computed by the browser and accessible via the Constraint Validation API.
formnovalidate can be placed on individual submit buttons to bypass validation for that button only (e.g. a "Save draft" button).
Angular's FormsModule patches the native form submit event and calls event.preventDefault() — the browser's built-in submit and validation never runs in Angular forms.
How Angular Validators Mirror Native Constraints

Angular's built-in Validators in @angular/forms are JavaScript implementations of the same rules as the Constraint Validation API. They produce error objects whose keys match the corresponding ValidityState flag names.
Custom Validation Messages in Plain HTML

The browser's built-in error messages are localized but not styleable and cannot match your design system. The standard pattern is to suppress the browser bubble with novalidate (or the invalid event), then display custom messages.

The invalid event fires on a form control when checkValidity() determines it is invalid. Calling event.preventDefault() suppresses the browser's default tooltip, giving you full control over the error display.
event bubbles — you can add one listener on the form element to catch all control errors via event delegation.
It fires once per call to
. If the user doesn't interact, the event fires on form submission.
on the form's submit event also prevents it.
Submit the form empty — every required field should highlight/report an error.
Test each constraint attribute individually: enter a value that violates only that rule.
Test boundary values for min/max: values at exactly min, max, min-1, and max+1.
Test the pattern with a value that looks almost right but fails (e.g. "abc" for email).
Check keyboard navigation: errors should be announced by a screen reader when the field receives focus.
Confirm that a valid submission goes through without triggering error messages.
Every time you re-validate, call setCustomValidity("") first. Otherwise a previous error flag persists even after the user fixes the issue.
Empty required fields match :invalid immediately. Use :user-invalid instead, or add a submitted/touched CSS class before styling.
Browser constraints are a UX aid. A user can disable them, modify the HTML, or send requests manually. Always validate on the server.
The pattern attribute implicitly anchors the regex (^ ... $). You do not add them yourself. Adding them duplicates anchors and may break the pattern.
maxlength does not work on type=number. Use max and step instead.
Angular adds novalidate automatically when form directives are active. You should still add it explicitly in non-Angular HTML forms where you handle validation yourself.
HTML validation attributes (required, minlength, maxlength, min, max, step, pattern, type) declare constraints declaratively on inputs.
The ValidityState object (input.validity) exposes a Boolean flag for each possible constraint failure; input.validity.valid is true only when all flags are false.
returns a boolean and fires the invalid event;
clears it — always clear before re-running checks.
CSS pseudo-classes (:valid, :invalid, :required, :user-invalid) reflect validation state; :user-invalid is preferred because it avoids errors on page load.
novalidate on the form suppresses browser bubbles but does not disable the Constraint Validation API — flags are still computed.
Angular automatically adds novalidate and provides Validators that mirror the native constraints (Validators.required, Validators.email, Validators.min, etc.).
Client-side validation is for UX only — always validate again on the server.
